subject: ap-chemistry
title: Colorimetric Analysis of Permanganate
stimulus: A student wants to determine the concentration of permanganate, $\text{MnO}_4^-(aq)$, in a solution. The student plans to use colorimetric analysis because solutions containing $\text{MnO}_4^-(aq)$ have a purple color.

parts.prompt: To determine the optimum wavelength for an experiment that measures the concentration of $\text{MnO}_4^-(aq)$, the student takes a sample of the solution and measures the amount of light absorbed by the sample over a range of wavelengths. The data are plotted in the graph shown. Identify the optimum wavelength that the student should use for the experimental procedure.
parts.scoring_notes: The optimum wavelength for colorimetric analysis is the wavelength of maximum absorbance ($\lambda_{max}$). For $\text{MnO}_4^-$, which appears purple (absorbs green/yellow light), the absorbance peak on the graph is at approximately 525 nm.

scoring_points.description: For identifying the wavelength of maximum absorbance from the graph (approximately 525 nm): 1 point

required_elements: ~525 nm (accept any value that correctly reads the maximum absorbance wavelength from the provided graph)
alternatives.correct_example: The optimum wavelength is approximately 525 nm, which corresponds to the peak absorbance in the graph. Using the wavelength of maximum absorbance maximizes sensitivity (greatest change in absorbance per change in concentration, per Beer's Law: $A = \varepsilon b c$).

wrong_examples: Choosing the wavelength of minimum absorbance — minimum absorbance means very little light is absorbed, making measurements less sensitive.
Choosing the wavelength of the complementary color — the optimum wavelength is determined by where absorbance is maximum, not by the color of the solution.
common_traps: Optimum wavelength = $\lambda_{max}$ (wavelength of maximum absorbance). This gives the steepest calibration curve and greatest analytical sensitivity. $\text{MnO}_4^-$ is purple → absorbs green light (~520–530 nm).

parts.prompt:
The student uses a stock solution of $2.40 \times 10^{-3}$ M $\text{KMnO}_4(aq)$ to prepare the standard solutions of $\text{MnO}_4^-(aq)$ that are needed to construct a calibration curve.

(i) The student uses a 100.0 mL graduated cylinder to measure a certain volume of $\text{KMnO}_4(aq)$ stock solution, as shown in the diagram given. What volume should the student record?

(ii) Calculate the volume, in mL, of $2.40 \times 10^{-3}$ M $\text{KMnO}_4(aq)$ that is required to produce 100.0 mL of a standard $1.68 \times 10^{-3}$ M $\text{MnO}_4^-(aq)$ solution.
parts.scoring_notes: (i) The graduated cylinder reading from the diagram shows 92.0 mL (read at the bottom of the meniscus). (ii) Using dilution equation: $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$. $(2.40 \times 10^{-3})(V_1) = (1.68 \times 10^{-3})(100.0)$. $V_1 = (1.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 100.0) / (2.40 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.168/0.00240 = 70.0$ mL.

scoring_points.description: For the correct volume reading from the graduated cylinder: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: The student should record 92.0 mL. For aqueous solutions in a graduated cylinder, the volume is read at the bottom of the curved meniscus.

wrong_examples: Reading at the top of the meniscus — for aqueous solutions, always read at the bottom of the meniscus.
Reporting 92 mL without the decimal — the graduated cylinder reads to the tenths place (92.0 mL).
common_traps: For water and aqueous solutions (concave meniscus), read the volume at the bottom of the meniscus. Report to the appropriate number of decimal places based on the glassware.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated volume using the dilution equation: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using $M_1/V_1 = M_2/V_2$ (incorrect form) — dilution: $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$ (moles are conserved on dilution).
Using 1.00 L instead of 100.0 mL — must keep units consistent (both in mL or both in L).
common_traps: Dilution: $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$. Moles of solute are conserved. $M_1$ = stock concentration, $V_1$ = volume of stock to take, $M_2$ = desired concentration, $V_2$ = final volume.

parts.prompt: Assuming that all lab equipment is functioning properly, identify which one of the procedural steps the student could have executed incorrectly that would explain why the marked data point is below the line of best fit. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: A data point below the line of best fit means the absorbance is lower than expected for that concentration — suggesting the effective concentration of $\text{MnO}_4^-$ in the cuvette was lower than prepared, or the cuvette was diluted. The most likely cause: the student did not rinse the cuvette with the standard solution (Step 3) before filling it, or improperly rinsed with distilled water and used a wet cuvette (diluting the sample). This gives a lower concentration in the cuvette than expected → lower absorbance → data point below the line.

scoring_points.description: For the correct step identification (Step 3, failure to rinse cuvette with the standard solution) with valid justification: 1 point

required_elements: If the cuvette was not rinsed with the standard solution (or was still wet from distilled water rinse), the standard solution would be diluted → lower actual concentration → lower absorbance than expected → data point below the line.
alternatives.correct_example: The student could have incorrectly executed Step 3. If the cuvette was not rinsed with the standard solution before filling, residual distilled water would remain in the cuvette, diluting the $\text{MnO}_4^-$ solution below its intended concentration. A lower concentration means lower absorbance (Beer's Law: $A = \varepsilon b c$), placing the data point below the calibration line.

wrong_examples: Citing Step 4 (measuring absorbance) — if the colorimeter is functioning properly, measurement error from Step 4 would not systematically cause low readings.
Citing Step 1 (preparing standard solutions) — if the stock solution preparation was wrong, all points would be affected, not just one.
common_traps: One data point below the line = lower absorbance than expected = effective concentration in cuvette was lower than prepared. The cuvette rinse step is the most likely single-point error: failing to rinse with the standard solution leaves diluting residues.
